dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for sick person.
Hi, I'm Jordan, an EMT with paramedic team 3. What made you call 911 today?
Ma'am, his brain seems out of it. I found him slumped in the car.
Can you open your eyes, please?
She's opening her eyes slowly with verbal stimuli.
Can you tell me what today's date is?
Squeeze my finger with each hand when I ask, okay?
Airway is clear but she has some drooling noted.
Is he breathing normally or is it labored?
Respiration is normal, no sounds of obstruction detected so far.
Let's get an O2 meter on him now.
Pulse is 74, regular, and strong with good capillary refill time.
Breathing at 20 per minute. Regular and adequate lung sounds bilaterally.
O2 saturation is at 98%. Things look okay right now with normal pattern.
Do you know of any symptoms you've been experiencing with him today?
He was confused a bit and sweating a lot, didn't make much sense when I talked to him earlier.
We can't find a medical history from the car. Do you know his medical history, by any chance?
He told me he had a heart attack and is on dialysis. I believe he has diabetes too. He also had a stroke in the past.
On a scale of 0 to 10, what would you say the level of discomfort is, if she is aware enough to tell us herself?
She hasn't exhibited any pain signs and hasn't verbalized discomfort during assessment. 0 is the best response and it fits.
She's clearly sweating and appears cool to the touch, but skin appears intact otherwise.
Let me shine a light in each eye to check reactivity. Pupils appear equal but slow to react.
Pupils are equal and reactive after light exposure, PERRL noted in both eyes.
Her stomach feels soft and not tender on palpation; no masses or rigidity detected.
There's a clean bandage on the arm. Looks like a dialysis access site, no signs of compromise.
Her blood sugar is low, so we will apply a finger stick for a reading.
Glucose reading is 61 milligrams per deciliter.
Taking her pulse now. Still 74 and regular with a strong pulse felt.
Her respiratory rate is sitting at 20 right now. Adequate but slightly elevated.
Taking her blood pressure now-120 systolic and 66 diastolic.
Her O2 level is stable at 98%.
Hooking her up to the EKG for baseline reading.
The EKG shows sinus rhythm. Consistent with normal findings on arrival.
We're also going to give her 15 grams of oral glucose.
I'll place an 18-gauge IV in the left arm to administer fluids if necessary.
Let's keep her in this position for comfort until we transport.
Based on her altered mental status and glucose level, activating protocol 3-15 for altered mental status.
Taking her pulse again now. Still 73 and with good rhythm.
Her respiratory rate has increased slightly to 22, still adequate.
Blood pressure is now 121 over 69. Still a stable blood pressure.
O2 saturation is still at a strong 98%. No added O2 needed.
Glucose has dropped to zero after meds, but improvement was observed in her mental status.
I'll repeat the EKG to ensure things haven't worsened.
Again, everything shows sinus rhythm. No signs of acute MI observed.
Blood pressure is now 109 over 65. Still stable within acceptable parameters.
O2 saturation has dropped slightly to 96%. Keep monitoring but no intervention required.
The EKG suggests an anterior infarct from prior events, but no acute problem observed.
There is a T wave abnormality in I/aVL/V5/V6. Suggestive of old ischemic changes.
Pulse is 79. Still regular and no signs of deterioration in her condition.
Ms. Yours, are you set on where you want to go for transport today?
[COMPANY_NAME] is our destination today.
We'll now secure the patient onto the stretcher for safe transport to [COMPANY_NAME].
Moving the patient now with the stretcher sheet. She's comfortable and not showing distress.
Patient is a 66-year-old female. Let's record that in the patient record.
Initial assessments and actions completed and noted for ER handover.
She is altered with a GCS of 13 (eye 3, verbal 4, motor 6).
She is diaphoretic but cool to the touch. Skin shows minimal luster.
Medical history includes stroke, MI, ESRD, and DM. No current meds or allergies noted.
12-lead showed conduction abnormalities like RBBB and fascicular block.
Results suggest old anterior infarct with some residual lateral ischemia.
A 4-lead was applied first, followed by 12-lead ECG as part of standard monitoring.
EKG was monitored repeatedly for any changes. No acute findings observed.
Glucose levels started at 61, then dropped after oral administration. Managed appropriately.
The patient denied pain and had a pain score of 0 throughout the encounter.
No oxygen therapy used-SpO2 remained in acceptable range.
Abdomen was assessed as soft, supple, and non-tender during secondary survey.
IV was placed using an 18g catheter in the left forearm for support.
The patient was transported in a comfortable position with no incident reported.
Patient care was transferred to an RN at [COMPANY_NAME] room 8 without issue.
Care was based on protocol, actions in compliance with EPCRs as noted.
The patient was found in the car, responsive but disoriented and diaphoretic.
Clean dressing on left arm with catheter at dialysis access.
Oral glucose given, responsiveness improved post-admin per EPCR.
Mental status was described as AAOx1 per GCS findings on scene.
No adverse reactions to glucose; the administration and response were protocol-guided.
The patient was kept in a stable and comfortable position for the majority of care.
EKG showed typical findings for left anterior fascicular block and RBBB.
No signs of new or acute MI found based on assessment and monitoring data.
We followed protocol 3-15 for altered mental status as directed by field impression.
Later O₂ was noted at 96, within acceptable limits and per field monitoring.
Patient was secured onto the stretcher using sheet assist, as per EPCR.
Continued monitoring showed no significant rhythm changes during transport.
The patient was diaphoretic and inactive, but maintained a stable mental status.
No external trauma or signs of injury were detected in secondary survey review.
Described as cool to touch with chronic pupil dilatation and profuse diaphoresis.
Blood sugar decreased clinically but no further intervention was needed.
Abdomen was reported as soft and non-tender according to EPCR documentation.
No need for oxygen-as per EPCR, it wasn't indicated based on clinical findings.
Glucose administered orally, procedure followed as dictated by patient condition.
Spinal precautions were not required per fields and adherence to protocol 3-15.
EKG supports prior history of MI and is not indicative of current hyperglycemia.
Despite altered status, the patient remained stable and monitored closely during transport.
Findings are consistent with histories of stroke, MI, and kidney disease.
No known allergies or medications identified during crew interaction.
The patient was responsive enough to be safely transported at this time.
The transport was smooth, with appropriate documentation of all events.
No O₂ therapy was needed-no clinical need for it was present during the mission.
Timely documentation and actions were taken during the scene and transport.
Transition of care was smooth, with no injuries to the patient or team.
All actions adhered to protocol 3-15 for medical complaint of altered mental status.
